FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacr.imcnto. CA 95814-2329 
Marilyn Craft 
4280 St Paul Circle 
Piw,burg, CA 9.t565 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A•l4-168 
Dear Ms. Craft: 
(916) 321-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
October 8, 201.t 
This letter re.,pond!-. to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provision..., 
of Government Code Section I 090.1 Please note that we do not provide advice on any other 
conflict of inten: . .,t restriction.,, if applicable. olll!-tidc the Act or Section I 090. We arc also not a 
finder of fact,, hen rendering advice (/11 re Oglt!,·hy ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), meaning that any 
advice we provide a .. !-tume., the facts the reque.,tcr provide .... to u .... arc complete and accurate. If 
thi..., is not the ca..,c, then our ad\'ice could be different. 
In regard..., to our adYice on Section I 090. we arc required to forn ard your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the rcque...,t to the Attorney General'!> Oflice and the Contra CoMa 
County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1 (c)(3 ).) We did not 
receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We arc also required to 
advise you that, for purpose!. of Section I 090. the following ad, ice ''is not admis!.iblc in a 
criminal proceeding again'it any individual other than the requc.,tor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Do the provbiom, of Section 1090 prohibit you from taking a ...,cat on the Pittsburg City 
Council where you and your .,pause are employee!. of nonprofit., that have existing contract!> with 
government cntitie!-1? 
CONCLUSION 
No. As explained below. the provision" under Section 1090 do not prohibit you from 
taking a seat on the Pittsburg City Council \\ here you and your ,pouse are employee!. of 
nonprofits that have existing contracts with government entitie'>. 
1 All statulcir) referenceY arc to the Go,crnmcnt Code. unh:Z, olhcrn ,[c rnd1ca1cd. 


FACTS 
File No. A-14-168 
Page No. 2 
You are the Executive Director for Open Opportunities, Inc., a 50l(c)(3) non-profit 
organization that oversees the day-to-day operations of a pre-apprentice training program for 
Future Build. In your capacity as an employee for Open Opportunities, you are a Program 
Manager for Future Build and receive a salary for this position, which is funded by the Contra 
Costa County Workforce Development Board. The contract for this job is between Open 
Opportunities and the Pittsburg Power Company who is the Fiscal Agent and administrator of 
Future Build under an agreement with the Contra Costa County Workforce Development Board. 
Your spouse, Jim Craft, is the Executive Director of Craft Community Care, a 50l(c)(3) 
nonprofit organization. One of its subsidiaries, STS Academy, has a contract with the City of 
Pittsburg for $80,000 for a one year period. This contract involves the provision of youth and 
teen services throughout the community that were previously provided by the City's Recreation 
Department. Craft Community Care has over 100 employees and Jim is a salaried employee. 
In addition, Craft Community Care also has a contract with the Pittsburg Unified School 
District to provide afterschool programs on its elementary and junior high school camprn,es. 
That contract has a one-year term for$ I ,255, 000. Finally, Craft Community Care has a contract 
with the State of California for the 21st Century High School ASSETs program. The term of the 
contract is from 2014-2019 and the funding comes from a grant that provides $350,000 annually. 
You also state that your daughter, Kolette Simonton, is a Senior Adminiďtration Analyst for the 
City of Pittsburg (Pittsburg Power Company). 
You are currently a candidate for a seat on the Pittsburg City Council. You would like to 
know if there will be any conflict of interest problems under Section 1090 that would arise if you 
are elected. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall 11• Taft (l 962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike 
at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. ( City of Imperial Beach 
11• Bailey (1980) l03Cal.App.3d 191, I 97.) 
Under Section 1090, the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official 
has a financial interest. (People 11• Honig ( 1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that 
violates Section 1090 is void. (Tf10msm1 11• Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition 
applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at 
pp. 646-649.) 


File No. A-14-168 
Page No. 3 
We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether, if elected, you will have 
a conflict of interest under Section I 090. 
Step One: Will you be subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are members." 
City Councils and their members are plainly covered by this prohibition. (See, e.g., Tlwmsmz, 
supra, at p. 645; City Council v. McKinley ( 1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 213.) Therefore, if 
elected, you and the Pittsburg City Council would be subject to the provisions of Section l 090. 
Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty .Gen. 34, 36 (200 I); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty .Gen. 230, 234 
( 1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections l 090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract.'" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) 
You have identified four contracts to which the nonprofit entities run by you and your 
spouse are parties. This step is thus satisfied. However, the only contract that would appear to 
raise any Section 1090 concerns, based on the Pittsburg City Council's authority to approve it, 
would be the contract between STS Academy and the City of Pittsburg. Thus, we do not address 
the others identified by your letter.2 
Step Three: Will you be making or participating in making a contract? 
As mentioned above, you are currently only a candidate for the Pittsburg City Council 
and, therefore, you did not make or participate in making the contract between STS and the City 
of Pittsburg (or any of the other contracts) while in an official capacity as a council member. 
The question then is whether the prohibitions under Section 1090 preclude you from taking a 
seat on the Pittsburg City Council subsequent to the execution of that contract. We do not 
believe they do. 
For example, in 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 176 (2002), a city council entered into a 40-year 
property lease with a nonprofit corporation. Eleven years into the lease, the nonprofit's salaried 
executive director was elected to the city council. (Id. at p. 177 .) In determining whether the 
city could continue leasing the property to the nonprofit, the Attorney General's opinion noted: 
At the time of the lease's execution, no one on the city council had 
a personal financial interest in the terms of the agreement. 
? In the event the Pittsburg City Council docs ha-.c the authority to approve any of the remaining contracts. 
the analysis in this lcucr can equally be applied to them. 


File No. A-14-168 
Page No. 4 
Accordingly, section 1090 had no application when the contract 
was executed. Because section l 090 is directed at the time a 
contract is .. made," it has no application now merely because the 
corporation's executive director has become a council member. 
(See City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (l 980) 103 Cal.App.3d 19 l, 
194-196; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 4 12, 426; 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 35-36 (200 I); 76 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 118, 
119-120 (1993); 73 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 191, 194-195 ( 1990).) 
(Ibid.) We agree with the reasoning and conclusion of this opinion. 
The facts here are similar in that the contract at issue was executed prior to your potential 
election to the Pittsburg City Council, and the provisions under Section 1090 will therefore not 
prohibit the continuation of the contract should you be elected. But if you are elected, you (and 
the entire City Council) may then have a prohibitory financial interest under Section 1090 in any 
modification, extension or renegotiation3 of that existing contract (or any new contract in which 
you have a financial interest) that the Pittsburg City Council must vote to approve, as discussed 
in the next step. 
Step Four: Do you have a financial interest in the contract? 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act b the making of a contract in which the official 
has a financial interest." (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Officials are deemed to have a 
financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section 
1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General 
opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may 
involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. 
(People 1•. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847,867, fn. 5; Terry v. Bender (1956) 143 
Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. l 58. 
161-162 (2001).) 
Your spouse is the Executive Director (and salaried employee) of a nonprofit 
organization that currently has a contract with the City of Pittsburg. The question then is 
whether you will have a financial interest in any contract between your spouse's nonprofit 
organization and the City of Pittsburg if you are elected to the City Council. As a general rule, a 
member of a board or commission always has a financial interest in his or her spouse's source of 
income for purposes of Section 1090. (See, e.g., 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 235 ( 1995).) Thus, 
if elected, both you and the City Council will have a prohibitory financial interest in such 
contracts unless an exception applies. 
J (Sec, e.g., City of Imperial Beac;/i 1•. Baile., ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d I 91 [exercising a renewal option and 
adjusting the payment rates is making a contract within the meaning of Section 1090J.) 


File No. A-14-168 
Page No. 5 
Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply? 
When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, 
the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain. Instead, the 
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 647-
649; Stigall, supra, alp. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 (2003); 70 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 45, 
48 (1987).) 
However, the Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's 
prohibition where the financial interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in 
Section 1091, or a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if ( 1) the officer in question 
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract lo the public agency, (2) such interest is 
noted in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the 
making of the contract. (Section 109 l(a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005); 83 
Ops.Cal.Alty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made 
without the officer's abstention, and generally, a noninleresl does not require disclosure. (City of 
Venzon v. Central Basin M1111. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).) 
Under the circumstances presented, we turn lo the "remote interest" specified in Section 
1091(b)(l): 
Thal of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from 
taxation pursuant to Section 501 (c)(3) of the Internal Revenue 
Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 50 l(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c)(5) of 
the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 50l(c)(5)). or a 
nonprofit corporation, except as provided in paragraph (8) of 
subdivision (a) of Section 1091.5. 
If elected, you would have a remote interest in any contract between the City of Pittsburg 
and STS Academy if you were an officer or employee of the nonprofit 50 I (c)(3) organization. 
However, it is your spouse that is the Executive Director of STS Academy so the question is 
whether the "remote interest" exception under Section l 091 (b )( 1) would extend to also apply to 
you. We believe that it does. 
Instructive on this issue is an Attorney General opinion (78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 230 
( 1995)) that is squarely on point. In that matter, a city council member's spouse was a partner in 
a law firm which represented a developer in various matters. (Id. at p. 231.) The developer 
came before the city council to enter into a development agreement with the city. (Ibid.) One of 
the central issues in the matter was whether the financial interest of the spouse in the 
development agreement would be attributed to the council member such that he would then have 
a "remote interest" in the development agreement: 


File No. A-14-168 
Page No. 6 
Under settled case law and opmtons of this office, a 
member of a board or commission always is financially interested 
in his or her spouse's source of income for purposes of section 
1090. This is true even if the husband and wife have an agreement 
that their own earnings are to be treated as their separate property, 
since each spouse is liable for the necessities of life for the other. 
(See Reece v. Alco/zolic Bev. Etc. Appeals Bd. ( 1976) 64 
Cal.App.3d 675, 683; Nielsen v. Richards ( 1925) 75 Cal.App. 680, 
685-687; 73 Ops.Cal.Atty. Gen. 191, 194-195 (1990); 69 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102, 106 (1986).) 
Accordingly the council member here would be financially 
interested in the development agreement by virtue of his wife's 
status as a partner in a law firm handling matters for the developer, 
even though the firm does not represent the developer regarding 
the proposed development. However, since no fees will be 
generated for the firm with respect to the development, the 
spouse's (a11d rims co1111cil member's) financial interest would 
meet the statutory definition of being "remote." 
(Id. at p. 235, emphasis added.) As a result, because the spouse's interest in the development 
agreement met the requirements for the "remote interest" exception under Section 109l(b)(6), 
the council member would thus have the same "remote interest." (See also 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
169.) In other words, since a spouse's property is attributed to the official. exemptions that 
would be applicable if the official possessed the interest directly also apply to the spouse's 
property. 
Here. if elected, you will have the same financial interest as your spouse in STS 
Academy. Accordingly, you will have a "remote interest" under Section 1091(b)( I) in any 
contract the Pittsburg City Council enters into with STS Academy.-1 Thus, so long as you do not 
participate in future City Council decisions on contracts with STS Academy and follow the other 
procedural steps outlined above, the board can make future contracts with STS Academy.5 
 Of course, you would also have the same "rcmolc mtcresl'' in any conlracts between 1hc City of Piw,burg 
and your nonprofit. Open Opponunities, Inc., and you \\ould be required to follow the same procedural slcps 
described above. 
5 As noted above, this include. any decision 10 modify, extend, or rcnegotialc any existing contracl with 
Music for Minors. (Sec. e.g., City of Imperial Bearh 1·. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 1 9 1  [exercising a renewal 
option and adjusting the payment rates is making a contract wi1hin the meaning of Scclion I 090),) 


JW:jgl 
File No. A-14-1 68 
Page No. 7 
If you have other questions on thi!:. matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
si e 
, egal Division

Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 50 l(c)(3)), pursuant to Section 501(c)(5) of 
the Internal Revenue Code (26 U.S.C. Sec. 50l(c)(5)). or a 
nonprofit corporation, except as provided in paragraph (8) of 
subdivision (a) of Section 1091.5.

property. 
Here. if elected, you will have the same financial interest as your spouse in STS 
Academy. Accordingly, you will have a "remote interest" under Section 1091(b)( I) in any 
contract the Pittsburg City Council enters into with STS Academy.-1 Thus, so long as you do not 
participate in future City Council decisions on contracts with STS Academy and follow the other 
procedural steps outlined above, the board can make future contracts with STS Academy.5 
 Of course, you would also have the same "rcmolc mtcresl'' in any conlracts between 1hc City of Piw,burg 
and your nonprofit. Open Opponunities, Inc., and you \\ould be required to follow the same procedural slcps 
described above.

Music for Minors. (Sec. e.g., City of Imperial Bearh 1·. Bailey ( 1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 1 9 1  [exercising a renewal 
option and adjusting the payment rates is making a contract wi1hin the meaning of Scclion I 090),)

QUESTION Do the provbiom, of Section 1090 prohibit you from taking a ...,cat on the Pittsburg City Council where you and your .,pause are employee!. of nonprofit., that have existing contract!> with government cntitie!-1? CONCLUSION No. As explained below. the provision" under Section 1090 do not prohibit you from taking a seat on the Pittsburg City Council \\ here you and your ,pouse are employee!. of nonprofits that have existing contracts with government entitie'>. 1 All statulcir) referenceY arc to the Go,crnmcnt Code. unh:Z, olhcrn ,[c rnd1ca1cd. FACTS File No. A-14-168 Page No. 2 You are the Executive Director for Open Opportunities, Inc., a 50l(c)(3) non-profit organization that oversees the day-to-day operations of a pre-apprentice training program for Future Build. In your capacity as an employee for Open Opportunities, you are a Program Manager for Future Build and receive a salary for this position, which is funded by the Contra Costa County Workforce Development Board. The contract for this job is between Open Opportunities and the Pittsburg Power Company who is the Fiscal Agent and administrator of Future Build under an agreement with the Contra Costa County Workforce Development Board. Your spouse, Jim Craft, is the Executive Director of Craft Community Care, a 50l(c)(3) nonprofit organization. One of its subsidiaries, STS Academy, has a contract with the City of Pittsburg for $80,000 for a one year period. This contract involves the provision of youth and teen services throughout the community that were previously provided by the City's Recreation Department. Craft Community Care has over 100 employees and Jim is a salaried employee.
